# Project Brief

## Project Name

`CLUS-LTROBS-2001 - From Deployment to Deep Insights: Mastering AI/ML with Cisco AI Pods & Splunk`

## Project Type

`4-hour instructor-led lab`

## Core Problem

Many AI/ML environments can be deployed, but far fewer can be operated with end-to-end visibility. Teams struggle to correlate infrastructure, platform, and workload behavior when troubleshooting performance, monitoring health, or optimizing expensive AI resources.

## Project Goal

Create a realistic Cisco Live lab package that teaches attendees how to:

- understand the lab topology and AI/ML environment
- validate access to the pre-staged Cisco AI Pods environment
- verify telemetry and operational data in Splunk
- create and interpret observability views
- investigate common AI/ML performance and health scenarios
- derive deeper operational insights from the environment

## Target Audience

- infrastructure architects
- platform engineers
- site reliability engineers
- observability practitioners
- AI/ML platform operators
- technically oriented Cisco Live attendees

Assume the audience is comfortable with enterprise infrastructure concepts but may have varying depth in Splunk, AI/ML operations, and Cisco AI Pods.

## Learning Model

This is not a greenfield build-from-zero infrastructure deployment lab.

The correct learning model is:

- the environment is pre-staged
- the lab teaches attendees how to operate and analyze it
- hands-on work focuses on validation, telemetry, observability, troubleshooting, and insight generation

## Lab Promise

By the end of the lab, attendees should be able to use Splunk to monitor and analyze telemetry from an AI/ML environment running on Cisco AI Pods, correlate infrastructure and workload behavior, investigate operational conditions that affect performance and reliability, and produce actionable observations.

## Tone Requirements

All generated artifacts must be:

- practical
- technical
- instructor-friendly
- credible for Cisco Live
- clear enough for attendees to follow live

Avoid:

- generic AI hype
- excessive product marketing language
- vague claims without steps, validation, or outcomes

## Narrative Arc

The lab should follow a realistic operator workflow:

1. Understand the environment
2. Validate platform and workload readiness
3. Verify data visibility in Splunk
4. Build operational views
5. Investigate a performance or health issue
6. Produce deeper insight and recommendations

## Lab Design Principles

- Optimize for reliable execution in a conference lab environment
- Prefer guided, deterministic tasks over fragile setup work
- Include expected results and validation checkpoints in every module
- Teach a repeatable method, not just a sequence of clicks
- Keep the technical bar credible without making the lab brittle

## Assumptions

Unless replaced by actual environment details later, assume:

- Cisco AI Pods infrastructure is already provisioned
- a sample AI/ML workload is already deployed
- Splunk is already available to attendees
- some data sources may already be connected, but validation is still part of the lab
- attendees have credentials and browser-based access to the required tools

## Primary Outputs

This project should produce at minimum:

- lab overview
- learning objectives
- lab topology description
- attendee lab guide
- instructor notes
- scenario descriptions
- slide deck outline
- artifact generation plan

This file defines the artifacts that should be created for the lab project and the purpose of each artifact.

### 1. Lab Guide

### 4. Topology and Scenario Guide

- `deliverables/lab-handout.md`

## Quality Bar

Every deliverable should:

- reference the exact session code and title
- align to the instructor-led lab format

- include realistic operational detail

## Content Constraints

1. access the environment
2. verify what is running
3. observe telemetry in Splunk
4. investigate behavior
5. derive insight

Anything that does not support that loop is secondary.

# Agent Build Instructions

This file is written for AI agents and human contributors who will generate the lab artifacts for this project.

## Mission

Create the source content for a realistic Cisco Live instructor-led lab:

- Session code: `CLUS-LTROBS-2001`
- Title: `From Deployment to Deep Insights: Mastering AI/ML with Cisco AI Pods & Splunk`
- Duration: `4 hours`
- Format: `Instructor-led lab`

The resulting content should be sufficient to produce a full lab package without requiring the original requester to restate the concept.

## Build Order

Create deliverables in this order:

1. topology and scenario guide
2. attendee lab guide
3. instructor guide
4. slide outline
5. build notes

This order is intentional. The scenario and topology definition should drive the rest of the content.

## Source-of-Truth Rules

Follow these rules while creating artifacts:

- Use the session code and title exactly as written
- Treat this project as an operational lab, not a keynote or breakout talk
- Assume a pre-staged environment unless concrete instructions say otherwise
- Prefer realistic workflows over ambitious but fragile setup tasks
- Include validation checkpoints and expected results in every hands-on module
- Keep content self-contained and executable by instructors

## Required Learning Flow

Every artifact should reinforce this sequence:

1. orient the attendee to the environment
2. validate access and baseline health
3. confirm telemetry visibility in Splunk
4. build or inspect observability views
5. investigate a guided operational issue
6. summarize deeper insights and recommendations

## Suggested Lab Modules

Agents should use these as the default module structure unless better environment details become available:

### Module 0: Introduction and environment orientation

Attendee outcome:

- understand the lab goal, tools, and topology

### Module 1: Validate the AI/ML environment

Attendee outcome:

- identify key components and confirm workload readiness

### Module 2: Verify telemetry in Splunk

Attendee outcome:

- confirm that relevant data is available for analysis

### Module 3: Build operational visibility

Attendee outcome:

- use Splunk searches, filters, or dashboards to understand system behavior

### Module 4: Investigate a performance or health scenario

Attendee outcome:

- perform guided troubleshooting using correlated data

### Module 5: Derive deeper insights

Attendee outcome:

- move from raw telemetry to operational recommendations

## Writing Standards

When generating attendee instructions:

- use numbered steps
- use direct action verbs
- include expected results after each exercise
- include short validation guidance when something could go wrong
- avoid large unexplained jumps between steps

When generating instructor notes:

- include module timing
- include the teaching point for each exercise
- include what a successful result looks like
- include recovery guidance for common issues

## Constraints

Do not assume attendees will:

- deploy a full AI cluster from scratch
- manually configure a large number of infrastructure primitives
- spend significant time on complex installation tasks

Do assume attendees can:

- access a browser-based lab environment
- log in to Splunk and related interfaces
- run guided searches
- inspect dashboards and data
- answer structured analysis questions

## Placeholder Policy

Use placeholders when exact technical details are not yet known, but do so explicitly.

Examples:

- `<SPLUNK_URL>`
- `<LAB_USERNAME>`
- `<WORKLOAD_NAME>`
- `<INDEX_NAME>`

Never hide missing information inside vague wording. If a detail is not yet known, mark it clearly as a placeholder.

## Deliverable Expectations

By default, generated markdown files should be:

- cleanly structured
- easy to scan in a live lab

+# Reference Notes
+
+This file captures the main external references and research conclusions used to shape this project.
+
+## Core Sources
+
+- Splunk Cisco AI POD setup overview:
+  [Set up data integrations for Cisco AI Pods](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-observability-cloud/observability-for-ai/splunk-ai-infrastructure-monitoring/set-up-data-integrations/cisco-ai-pods)
+- Splunk Cisco AI POD monitoring guide:
+  [Monitor the performance of Cisco AI PODs](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-observability-cloud/observability-for-ai/splunk-ai-infrastructure-monitoring/monitor-and-troubleshoot-your-ai-infrastructure/monitor-the-performance-of-cisco-ai-pods)
+- Splunk AI Agent Monitoring setup:
+  [Set up AI Agent Monitoring](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-observability-cloud/observability-for-ai/splunk-ai-agent-monitoring/set-up-ai-agent-monitoring)
+- Splunk workshop material:
+  [Monitoring Cisco AI Pods with Splunk Observability Cloud](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/index.print.html)
+- Splunk example collector configuration:
+  [splunk-opentelemetry-examples Cisco AI ready pods config](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/signalfx/splunk-opentelemetry-examples/main/collector/cisco-ai-ready-pods/base-otel-collector-config/values.yaml)
+- Cisco AI POD repository:
+  [Cisco-AI-POD GitHub repository](https://github.com/ucs-compute-solutions/Cisco-AI-POD)
+
+## What The Sources Indicate
+
+- Splunk supports Cisco AI POD-oriented monitoring through the Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector and related integrations.
+- Splunk documentation describes built-in Cisco AI POD dashboard experiences.
+- Splunk workshop material demonstrates that hands-on experiences can be created without access to a physical Cisco AI POD.
+- Splunk example configurations expose useful metric families and naming patterns for compatibility targeting.
+- The Cisco AI POD repository provides architectural context for the kind of environment being modeled, even if the lab does not deploy that stack directly.
+
+## Feasibility Conclusion
+
+It is feasible to create a datagen that simulates a Cisco AI Pods-style environment for lab purposes.
+
+Strongly feasible:
+
+- simulate GPU, host, storage, and application behavior
+- generate token, latency, and concurrency metrics for inference workloads
+- create realistic troubleshooting scenarios with correlated telemetry
+- populate lab-built dashboards and many metric-driven built-in experiences
+
+Needs validation:
+
+- full parity with every built-in Cisco AI POD dashboard tab
+- exact compatibility for Cisco UCS and Cisco Nexus-specific views
+- any feature that depends on undocumented dimensions, metadata, or chart queries
+
+## Monitoring Scope Distinction
+
+This project should keep two Splunk concepts separate:
+
+- `AI Infrastructure Monitoring`
+- `AI Agent Monitoring`
+
+AI Infrastructure Monitoring is the main target for the lab and datagen.
+
+AI Agent Monitoring requires additional application instrumentation, including traces and metric types expected by the Agents page. Infrastructure-only telemetry is not sufficient for that experience.
+
+## Practical Lab Implication
+
+The lab should claim that it uses a simulated Cisco AI Pods-style environment and aims to populate Splunk experiences as closely as possible. It should not claim that attendees are operating a physical Cisco AI POD unless the environment changes later.
+
+## Recommended Project Posture
+
+- build a high-quality synthetic environment first
+- validate against a real Splunk Observability tenant
+- record mismatches in build notes
+- iterate toward better dashboard compatibility over time
*** End Patch

# Reference Notes

This file captures the main external references and research conclusions used to shape this project.

## Core Sources

- Splunk Cisco AI POD setup overview:
  [Set up data integrations for Cisco AI Pods](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-observability-cloud/observability-for-ai/splunk-ai-infrastructure-monitoring/set-up-data-integrations/cisco-ai-pods)
- Splunk Cisco AI POD monitoring guide:
  [Monitor the performance of Cisco AI PODs](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-observability-cloud/observability-for-ai/splunk-ai-infrastructure-monitoring/monitor-and-troubleshoot-your-ai-infrastructure/monitor-the-performance-of-cisco-ai-pods)
- Splunk AI Agent Monitoring setup:
  [Set up AI Agent Monitoring](https://help.splunk.com/en/splunk-observability-cloud/observability-for-ai/splunk-ai-agent-monitoring/set-up-ai-agent-monitoring)
- Splunk workshop material:
  [Monitoring Cisco AI Pods with Splunk Observability Cloud](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/index.print.html)
- Splunk example collector configuration:
  [splunk-opentelemetry-examples Cisco AI ready pods config](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/signalfx/splunk-opentelemetry-examples/main/collector/cisco-ai-ready-pods/base-otel-collector-config/values.yaml)
- Cisco AI POD repository:
  [Cisco-AI-POD GitHub repository](https://github.com/ucs-compute-solutions/Cisco-AI-POD)

## What The Sources Indicate

- Splunk supports Cisco AI POD-oriented monitoring through the Splunk Distribution of OpenTelemetry Collector and related integrations.
- Splunk documentation describes built-in Cisco AI POD dashboard experiences.
- Splunk workshop material demonstrates that hands-on experiences can be created without access to a physical Cisco AI POD.
- Splunk example configurations expose useful metric families and naming patterns for compatibility targeting.
- The Cisco AI POD repository provides architectural context for the kind of environment being modeled, even if the lab does not deploy that stack directly.

## Feasibility Conclusion

It is feasible to create a datagen that simulates a Cisco AI Pods-style environment for lab purposes.

Strongly feasible:

- simulate GPU, host, storage, and application behavior
- generate token, latency, and concurrency metrics for inference workloads
- create realistic troubleshooting scenarios with correlated telemetry
- populate lab-built dashboards and many metric-driven built-in experiences

Needs validation:

- full parity with every built-in Cisco AI POD dashboard tab
- exact compatibility for Cisco UCS and Cisco Nexus-specific views
- any feature that depends on undocumented dimensions, metadata, or chart queries

## Monitoring Scope Distinction

This project should keep two Splunk concepts separate:

- `AI Infrastructure Monitoring`
- `AI Agent Monitoring`

AI Infrastructure Monitoring is the main target for the lab and datagen.

AI Agent Monitoring requires additional application instrumentation, including traces and metric types expected by the Agents page. Infrastructure-only telemetry is not sufficient for that experience.

## Practical Lab Implication

The lab should claim that it uses a simulated Cisco AI Pods-style environment and aims to populate Splunk experiences as closely as possible. It should not claim that attendees are operating a physical Cisco AI POD unless the environment changes later.

## Recommended Project Posture

- build a high-quality synthetic environment first
- validate against a real Splunk Observability tenant
- record mismatches in build notes
- iterate toward better dashboard compatibility over time

Output:
# Project Brief

## Project Name

`CLUS-LTROBS-2001 - From Deployment to Deep Insights: Mastering AI/ML with Cisco AI Pods & Splunk`

## Project Type

`4-hour instructor-led lab`

## Core Problem

Many AI/ML environments can be deployed, but far fewer can be operated with end-to-end visibility. Teams struggle to correlate infrastructure, platform, and workload behavior when troubleshooting performance, monitoring health, or optimizing expensive AI resources.

## Project Goal

Create a realistic Cisco Live lab package that teaches attendees how to:

- understand the lab topology and simulated AI/ML environment
- validate access to the synthetic Cisco AI Pods-style environment
- verify telemetry and operational data in Splunk
- create and interpret observability views
- investigate common AI/ML performance and health scenarios
- derive deeper operational insights from the environment

## Target Audience

- infrastructure architects
- platform engineers
- site reliability engineers
- observability practitioners
- AI/ML platform operators
- technically oriented Cisco Live attendees

Assume the audience is comfortable with enterprise infrastructure concepts but may have varying depth in Splunk, AI/ML operations, and Cisco AI Pods.

## Learning Model

This is not a greenfield build-from-zero infrastructure deployment lab.

The correct learning model is:

- the environment is synthetic and pre-staged for the attendee
- the environment is modeled after a Cisco AI Pods deployment
- the lab teaches attendees how to operate and analyze it
- hands-on work focuses on validation, telemetry, observability, troubleshooting, and insight generation

## Lab Promise

By the end of the lab, attendees should be able to use Splunk to monitor and analyze telemetry from a simulated AI/ML environment modeled after Cisco AI Pods, correlate infrastructure and workload behavior, investigate operational conditions that affect performance and reliability, and produce actionable observations.

## Tone Requirements

All generated artifacts must be:

- practical
- technical
- instructor-friendly
- credible for Cisco Live
- clear enough for attendees to follow live

Avoid:

- generic AI hype
- excessive product marketing language
- vague claims without steps, validation, or outcomes

## Narrative Arc

The lab should follow a realistic operator workflow:

1. Understand the environment
2. Validate platform and workload readiness
3. Verify data visibility in Splunk
4. Build operational views
5. Investigate a performance or health issue
6. Produce deeper insight and recommendations

## Lab Design Principles

- Optimize for reliable execution in a conference lab environment
- Prefer guided, deterministic tasks over fragile setup work
- Include expected results and validation checkpoints in every module
- Teach a repeatable method, not just a sequence of clicks
- Keep the technical bar credible without making the lab brittle
- Simulate the telemetry contract rather than the physical hardware
- Use internally consistent and causally related data rather than random values

## Assumptions

Unless replaced by actual environment details later, assume:

- no physical Cisco AI POD hardware is available in the lab
- a synthetic telemetry generator provides Cisco AI Pods-style data
- a sample AI/ML workload model is represented by the datagen
- Splunk is already available to attendees
- some data sources may already be connected, but validation is still part of the lab
- attendees have credentials and browser-based access to the required tools

## Technical Strategy

The lab should be built around a multi-tenant LLM inference simulation, not a generic infrastructure simulator.

Recommended logical services:

- `customer-support-assistant`
- `document-summarizer`
- `code-assistant`
- optional background workload: `nightly-embedding-batch`

The simulated environment should emit correlated signals across:

- application and model-serving behavior
- GPU utilization and GPU memory pressure
- host and platform health
- storage latency and throughput
- network throughput and errors

## Monitoring Scope

This project should explicitly distinguish between:

- `AI Infrastructure Monitoring`
- `AI Agent Monitoring`

AI Infrastructure Monitoring is the primary lab focus.

AI Agent Monitoring can be included as an optional extension only if the project also emits the required traces and metrics for instrumented AI applications. Infrastructure metrics alone are not sufficient for the Splunk Agents page or related APM-style experiences.

## Validation Expectations

The project can credibly target Splunk's out-of-the-box Cisco AI POD experiences, but exact parity with a real Cisco AI POD should not be assumed without validation.

Use this phrasing consistently in artifacts:

- the datagen is intended to populate built-in dashboards as closely as possible
- full dashboard parity must be validated in a real Splunk Observability environment
- undocumented chart requirements may require iterative schema adjustments

## Primary Outputs

This project should produce at minimum:

- lab overview
- learning objectives
- lab topology description
- attendee lab guide
- instructor notes
- scenario descriptions
- slide deck outline
- artifact generation plan
- datagen design and implementation guidance
- validation notes for dashboard population

I updated the project files to reflect the actual plan from our chat: synthetic environment, datagen-first lab design, workload choice, validation boundaries, and the distinction between infrastructure monitoring and AI Agent Monitoring.

The main changes are in [README.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/README.md), [docs/PROJECT_BRIEF.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/docs/PROJECT_BRIEF.md), [docs/ARTIFACT_PLAN.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/docs/ARTIFACT_PLAN.md), [docs/AGENT_BUILD_INSTRUCTIONS.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/docs/AGENT_BUILD_INSTRUCTIONS.md), [docs/DATAGEN_PLAN.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/docs/DATAGEN_PLAN.md), and [docs/DATAGEN_BUILD_GUIDE.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/docs/DATAGEN_BUILD_GUIDE.md). I also added [docs/REFERENCE_NOTES.md](/Users/mkuglerr/code2/codex_projects/ai-pods/docs/REFERENCE_NOTES.md) to capture the Splunk docs, the Cisco AI POD repo, and the feasibility conclusions.

What changed in substance:
- the lab now explicitly assumes `no physical Cisco AI POD`
- the core environment is a `simulated Cisco AI Pods-style` deployment
- the preferred workload is a `multi-tenant LLM inference` platform
- the docs now state that built-in dashboard population is a `compatibility target`, not a guaranteed outcome
- `AI Infrastructure Monitoring` and `AI Agent Monitoring` are now clearly separated
- the artifact plan now includes a `datagen spec` and reference notes for future agents

I also verified the updated wording is present across the repo.

The next useful step is to create the first implementation-facing artifact: `deliverables/datagen-spec.md`, with concrete metric families, dimensions, scenarios, and an MVP schema for Splunk.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message:
Yes. Reusing that workshop is the pragmatic move.

The key point is that the workshop already solves the exact credibility problem you have: it explicitly says it gives hands-on experience for monitoring Cisco AI PODs “without requiring access to an actual Cisco AI POD” and includes OTel Collector deployment, Prometheus receiver setup, Weaviate deployment, Python LLM app instrumentation, and review of the built-in Cisco AI POD dashboards ([workshop overview](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/14-cisco-ai-pods/index.html), [environment overview](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/14-cisco-ai-pods/2-workshop/1-overview-of-workshop-environment/index.html)). It also shows a realistic lab model: shared OpenShift in AWS, pre-deployed NIM models, Weaviate, and a Prometheus exporter that simulates Pure Storage metrics ([environment overview](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/14-cisco-ai-pods/2-workshop/1-overview-of-workshop-environment/index.html)).

What this means for you:
- Reuse the workshop structure and learning flow.
- Reuse the setup patterns for OTel Collector and dashboard review.
- Reuse the idea of simulated component metrics instead of building a full fake AI POD from scratch.
- Keep your custom datagen narrower: focus on whatever the workshop does not already simulate well.

What you can likely reuse directly:
- lab sequence
- instructor-led module flow
- OTel Collector deployment/config steps
- Prometheus receiver pattern
- built-in dashboard review flow for `Cisco AI PODs Dashboard` and `PURE STORAGE` tab ([dashboard review](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/14-cisco-ai-pods/2-workshop/7-review-ai-pod-dashboards/index.html))
- Weaviate / LLM application observability storyline

What you still need to adapt:
- your Cisco Live session framing and branding
- a 4-hour agenda instead of their shorter workshop pacing
- any environment details you do not actually have, especially OpenShift, GPUs, NIM, and Weaviate
- your explicit “simulated Cisco AI Pods-style environment” language
- a fallback datagen for missing telemetry sources

The main constraint is that the workshop is not pure datagen. It assumes some real shared infrastructure: OpenShift on AWS, NVIDIA GPUs, NIM models, and Weaviate. So if you do not have those, you should reuse the workshop as the lab blueprint, not as a drop-in deployment.

My recommendation:
- Treat the Splunk workshop as your base lab.
- Fork the flow, terminology, and instructor mechanics.
- Replace missing infrastructure pieces with your datagen.
- Keep the built-in dashboard validation steps.
- Position your lab as “adapted from the Splunk Cisco AI POD workshop model for a simulated environment.”

One caution: if you plan to republish or heavily copy the workshop text/images externally, verify the usage terms and attribution expectations. The public `splunk/observability-workshop` repo appears under Apache 2.0 in Splunk’s GitHub org listing, but I would still verify the exact repo/license before reusing content verbatim ([Splunk GitHub org listing](https://github.com/orgs/splunk/repositories)).

If you want, I can update your repo now to pivot the project around “reuse the Splunk Cisco AI POD workshop as the base lab, with targeted datagen extensions where infrastructure is unavailable.”

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: i want to use the workshop as is, assuming we get all the shared infra for every student how can we adapt? if we move away from the datagen approach. Also what could we improve for our presentation?

payload.message: i want to use the workshop as is, assuming we get all the shared infra for every student how can we adapt? if we move away from the datagen approach. Also what could we improve for our presentation?

I’m mapping the workshop into a Cisco Live lab format now so I can tell you what to keep unchanged, what to wrap with your own guide, and where to improve the presentation value without rebuilding the technical content.

content.text: I’m mapping the workshop into a Cisco Live lab format now so I can tell you what to keep unchanged, what to wrap with your own guide, and where to improve the presentation value without rebuilding the technical content.

payload.message:
Yes. If you can provide the shared infra per student, I would drop the datagen plan and use the Splunk workshop as the technical core.

The workshop is already designed for “no physical Cisco AI POD” use, but still gives hands-on work with OpenShift, OTel Collector, Prometheus receivers, Weaviate, Python LLM instrumentation, and review of AI POD dashboards ([workshop overview](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/14-cisco-ai-pods/index.html), last modified January 19, 2026). That is much closer to a production-ready lab than building your own simulator.

**How to adapt it**
Use the workshop `as-is` for the lab mechanics, and add your own Cisco Live wrapper around it.

Recommended structure:
1. Keep their setup and lab flow unchanged.
2. Add your own intro module:
   - session objectives
   - architecture overview
   - what is simulated vs real in the shared infra
   - why this matters for AI operations
3. Add your own instructor guide:
   - timing per section
   - checkpoints
   - recovery notes
   - talking points
4. Add your own debrief module after each major step:
   - what signal was collected
   - what operational question it answers
   - why an operator cares
5. Add your own wrap-up:
   - key monitoring concepts
   - troubleshooting workflow
   - “what to monitor first” checklist

**What to keep from the workshop**
These are the high-value parts to preserve:
- deploy OTel Collector in OpenShift
- add Prometheus receivers for infra metrics
- review NVIDIA / storage / AI POD dashboards
- review LLM application behavior
- instrument the LLM application
- review metrics, traces, and logs

That sequence already teaches both infrastructure monitoring and application observability.

**How to present it as your Cisco Live lab**
Do not present it as “we reused a workshop.”
Present it as:

“`CLUS-LTROBS-2001` is a hands-on instructor-led lab built on a shared Cisco AI POD-style observability environment using Splunk Observability Cloud.”

That is accurate and stronger.

**What to improve for your presentation**
The workshop gives you the hands-on flow. Your improvements should be around framing and teaching value.

Best upgrades:
- Add a clear operator journey:
  `environment -> telemetry -> dashboards -> root cause -> insight`
- Add one architecture slide up front:
  OpenShift, OTel Collector, Prometheus exporters, Weaviate, LLM app, Splunk
- Add one “signal map” slide:
  GPU, storage, app, traces, logs, dashboards
- Add explicit learning outcomes on slide 1
- Add checkpoint questions after each section
- Add 2-3 guided incidents for discussion:
  - latency spike
  - storage slowdown
  - bad model response path / app slowdown
- Add a final “production lessons” slide:
  - what is easy in lab
  - what changes in real enterprise rollout

**Best 4-hour adaptation**
A good agenda would be:
- 20 min: intro, architecture, goals
- 40 min: access environment and collector setup
- 45 min: infra monitoring and dashboard review
- 45 min: vector DB / workload visibility
- 50 min: LLM app instrumentation and deployment
- 25 min: metrics, traces, logs walkthrough
- 15 min: operational debrief and Q&A

**My recommendation**
If the shared infra is real and stable, this is the right move. The workshop removes most of the engineering risk and lets you spend your effort on:
- Cisco Live packaging
- instructor notes
- cleaner narrative
- stronger teaching moments

If you want, I can now update your repo to pivot away from datagen and toward:
- “workshop reuse as primary lab”
- adapted 4-hour agenda
- instructor guide outline
- presentation improvements list

- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: what is the cheapest way to get gpus for each student in their own ec2?

payload.message: what is the cheapest way to get gpus for each student in their own ec2?

I’m checking current AWS GPU instance options and pricing from official sources first. Then I’ll translate that into the cheapest per-student lab pattern, including where dedicated-per-student GPUs stop making economic sense.

content.text: I’m checking current AWS GPU instance options and pricing from official sources first. Then I’ll translate that into the cheapest per-student lab pattern, including where dedicated-per-student GPUs stop making economic sense.

action.query: site:aws.amazon.com EC2 On-Demand Pricing G4dn us-east-1
queries: site:aws.amazon.com EC2 On-Demand Pricing G4dn us-east-1
site:aws.amazon.com EC2 On-Demand Pricing G6 us-east-1
site:aws.amazon.com EC2 instance types g4dn g5 g6 pricing
site:aws.amazon.com "Amazon EC2 G6f instances" price hr

action.query: site:docs.aws.amazon.com EC2 Spot Instances overview official AWS
queries: site:docs.aws.amazon.com EC2 Spot Instances overview official AWS
site:docs.aws.amazon.com EC2 Spot interruptions GPU official AWS

payload.message:
If you mean `one GPU-backed EC2 instance per student`, the cheapest answer and the cheapest viable answer are different.

The absolute cheapest GPU EC2 I found is `g6f.large`, a fractional-GPU instance with `1/8` of an NVIDIA L4 and `3 GB` of GPU memory. AWS says G6f is their fractional-GPU family and is available in `us-east-1`, `us-east-2`, and `sa-east-1` among others ([AWS announcement, July 29, 2025](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2025/07/amazon-ec2-g6f-instances-fractional-gpus/), [AWS announcement, Nov 13, 2025](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2025/11/amazon-ec2-g6f-instances-additional-regions/)). Public price catalogs currently show about `$0.202/hour` on-demand for `g6f.large` in `us-east-1` ([Vantage](https://instances.vantage.sh/aws/ec2/g6f.large)).

But for your workshop, I would not plan around `g6f.large`. That is probably too small for a realistic Splunk Cisco AI POD lab that uses GPU-backed AI services. This is an inference from the published specs: `g6f.large` only has `3 GB` of GPU memory, while the workshop environment Splunk documents uses shared OpenShift with NVIDIA GPUs and pre-deployed NIM models ([workshop environment](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/index.print.html)). That points to a real GPU-serving environment, not tiny fractional slices.

For a `cheapest viable per-student dedicated GPU EC2`, I would narrow it to:

- `g4dn.xlarge`
  - `1 x NVIDIA T4`, `16 GiB` GPU memory ([AWS G4 specs](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/instance-types/g4/))
  - about `$0.526/hour` on-demand in `us-east-1` ([Vantage](https://instances.vantage.sh/aws/ec2/g4dn.xlarge))
  - cheapest full-GPU x86 option I found
- `g6.xlarge`
  - `1 x NVIDIA L4`, `24 GiB` GPU memory ([AWS accelerated computing specs](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/instance-types/accelerated-computing/))
  - about `$0.805/hour` on-demand in `us-east-1` ([Vantage](https://instances.vantage.sh/aws/ec2/g6.xlarge))
  - safer than `g4dn.xlarge` for modern inference workloads

My recommendation:
- If you only care about lowest cost and can tolerate risk: test `g4dn.xlarge`.
- If you want the cheapest option that is more likely to behave well for AI inference demos: use `g6.xlarge`.
- If you want the actual cheapest price per student regardless of viability: `g6f.large`, but I would not bet the lab on it.

For a 4-hour lab, rough on-demand instance cost per student in `us-east-1` is:
- `g6f.large`: about `$0.81`
- `g4dn.xlarge`: about `$2.10`
- `g6.xlarge`: about `$3.22`

For 30 students, that is roughly:
- `g6f.large`: `$24.24`
- `g4dn.xlarge`: `$63.12`
- `g6.xlarge`: `$96.60`

That excludes EBS, NAT, and data transfer.

One important warning: using `Spot` would be cheaper, and AWS confirms Spot is discounted versus on-demand, but Spot instances can be interrupted at any time ([Spot overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-spot-instances.html), [Spot interruptions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/spot-interruptions.html)). For a live Cisco lab, I would avoid Spot unless you have a fallback plan.

The cheapest architecture overall is still `not` one GPU EC2 per student. It is one shared GPU/OpenShift environment with a dedicated namespace per student, which is exactly how the Splunk workshop is designed ([workshop environment](https://splunk.github.io/observability-workshop/en/ninja-workshops/index.print.html)). That will usually be much cheaper and much easier to operate.

If you want, I can next do a costed design comparison:
- `shared cluster`
- `one GPU EC2 per student`
- `hybrid model with a few shared GPU nodes and per-student non-GPU bastions`

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

content.text: but if we get a shared cluster, each student should be able to configure it is own collector, is this something we can do? how can we run this with a single openshift cluster and make sure everyone learns how to setup monitoring piece and explore the dashboards

payload.message: but if we get a shared cluster, each student should be able to configure it is own collector, is this something we can do? how can we run this with a single openshift cluster and make sure everyone learns how to setup monitoring piece and explore the dashboards

Yes. A single shared OpenShift cluster is the right model, and each student can still configure “their own collector” if you scope it correctly.

The key is: do not give each student cluster-wide ownership. Give each student an isolated namespace and a pre-approved collector deployment pattern.

**What works**

Use one shared cluster with:

- one namespace per student, like `lab-student-01`
- one service account per student
- RBAC limited to that namespace
- one pre-created Splunk access token or secret per student
- one collector instance per student, deployed into their namespace
- one sample app per student, also in their namespace

That lets every student:
- deploy or modify their own collector
- configure receivers/processors/exporters
- scrape only their own app/services
- send data with student-specific attributes
- explore dashboards filtered to their namespace or student tag

**What should stay shared**

Keep these shared to reduce risk:

- the OpenShift cluster itself
- GPU nodes
- base operators
- shared model-serving components if needed
- shared ingress/domain setup
- optional shared infra dashboards

So the students learn the monitoring setup, but you avoid giving 30 people permission to break the platform.

**Best lab pattern**

Use a two-layer model:

1. `Shared platform layer`
   - OpenShift
   - GPU infrastructure
   - cluster services
   - maybe shared NIM / shared model backend

2. `Per-student observability layer`
   - student namespace
   - student collector
   - student app or demo workload
   - student-specific labels/attributes

That is how you keep it teachable.

**How each student learns collector setup**

Each student can still do real setup tasks:

1. log into OpenShift
2. switch to their namespace
3. inspect the sample app/service endpoints
4. deploy their collector using a provided YAML or Helm values file
5. add or enable Prometheus receivers
6. configure OTLP export to Splunk
7. add attributes like `student.id`, `k8s.namespace.name`, `service.name`
8. verify data in Splunk
9. explore dashboards and filter to their own telemetry

That is real learning, even in a shared cluster.

**How to avoid collisions**

You need strict isolation rules:

- namespace-scoped RBAC only
- unique collector names per student
- unique service names or labels per student
- separate secrets per student
- dashboard filters based on namespace, service, or `student.id`
- quotas to prevent runaway workloads

Without this, students will overwrite each other or mix telemetry.

**How dashboard exploration should work**

There are two good options.

Option 1: `Shared dashboards with filters`
- all data lands in the same Splunk org
- dashboards are filtered by `k8s.namespace.name` or `student.id`
- simplest operationally

Option 2: `Per-student dashboard copies`
- each student gets their own dashboard view
- more isolation
- more setup overhead

I would use Option 1 unless you have a strong reason not to.

**What to pre-stage**

To keep a 4-hour lab reliable, pre-stage:

- cluster and namespaces
- student credentials
- sample app manifests
- secret placeholders
- collector base manifest
- any cluster-level permissions already approved
- GPU-backed services if required

Let students perform the observability-specific work, not the fragile platform bootstrapping.

**What students should still do themselves**

Make them do the high-value steps:

- deploy or update the collector
- enable the right receivers
- validate ingestion
- inspect metrics/logs/traces
- troubleshoot a scenario
- explain what the dashboards mean

That preserves hands-on learning.

**Recommended architecture**

A practical design is:

- one OpenShift cluster
- one shared GPU/model layer
- 20-40 student namespaces
- one collector deployment per namespace
- one demo app per namespace
- one Splunk org
- one set of dashboards filtered by namespace/student

**Main risk**

The biggest risk is not technical feasibility. It is scale and noise:
- too many collectors
- too much telemetry volume
- students confusing shared signals with their own

You solve that with:
- namespace scoping
- per-student labels
- sampling limits
- dashboard filters
- a clear lab guide

**Bottom line**

Yes, a single OpenShift cluster can support this well. The right model is not “each student owns a cluster.” It is “each student owns a namespace-scoped observability setup inside a shared cluster.”

If you want, I can draft the exact lab architecture and the namespace/RBAC/collector model for 20-40 students next.

2. `Per-student observability layer`
   - student namespace
   - student collector
   - student app or demo workload
   - student-specific labels/attributes

That is how you keep it teachable.

**How each student learns collector setup**

Each student can still do real setup tasks:

1. log into OpenShift
2. switch to their namespace
3. inspect the sample app/service endpoints
4. deploy their collector using a provided YAML or Helm values file
5. add or enable Prometheus receivers
6. configure OTLP export to Splunk
7. add attributes like `student.id`, `k8s.namespace.name`, `service.name`
8. verify data in Splunk
9. explore dashboards and filter to their own telemetry

That is real learning, even in a shared cluster.

**How to avoid collisions**

You need strict isolation rules:

- namespace-scoped RBAC only
- unique collector names per student
- unique service names or labels per student
- separate secrets per student
- dashboard filters based on namespace, service, or `student.id`
- quotas to prevent runaway workloads

Without this, students will overwrite each other or mix telemetry.

**How dashboard exploration should work**

There are two good options.

Option 1: `Shared dashboards with filters`
- all data lands in the same Splunk org
- dashboards are filtered by `k8s.namespace.name` or `student.id`
- simplest operationally

Option 2: `Per-student dashboard copies`
- each student gets their own dashboard view
- more isolation
- more setup overhead

I would use Option 1 unless you have a strong reason not to.

**What to pre-stage**

To keep a 4-hour lab reliable, pre-stage:

- cluster and namespaces
- student credentials
- sample app manifests
- secret placeholders
- collector base manifest
- any cluster-level permissions already approved
- GPU-backed services if required

Let students perform the observability-specific work, not the fragile platform bootstrapping.

**What students should still do themselves**

Make them do the high-value steps:

- deploy or update the collector
- enable the right receivers
- validate ingestion
- inspect metrics/logs/traces
- troubleshoot a scenario
- explain what the dashboards mean

That preserves hands-on learning.

**Recommended architecture**

A practical design is:

- one OpenShift cluster
- one shared GPU/model layer
- 20-40 student namespaces
- one collector deployment per namespace
- one demo app per namespace
- one Splunk org
- one set of dashboards filtered by namespace/student

**Main risk**

The biggest risk is not technical feasibility. It is scale and noise:
- too many collectors
- too much telemetry volume
- students confusing shared signals with their own

You solve that with:
- namespace scoping
- per-student labels
- sampling limits
- dashboard filters
- a clear lab guide

**Bottom line**

Yes, a single OpenShift cluster can support this well. The right model is not “each student owns a cluster.” It is “each student owns a namespace-scoped observability setup inside a shared cluster.”

If you want, I can draft the exact lab architecture and the namespace/RBAC/collector model for 20-40 students next.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the image.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use directives
- Only use ::automation-update{...} when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and emit view-mode directives (mode="view") for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using automation directives (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Directive format
- Modes: view, suggested update, suggested create. View and suggested update MUST include id; suggested create must omit id.
- For view directives, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested update/create, include name, prompt, rrule, cwds, and status. cwds can be a comma-separated list or a JSON array string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.
- Directives should be on their own line(s) and be separated by newlines.
- Do not generate remark directives with multiline attribute values.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces it should use (if any), then map those answers into name/prompt/rrule/cwds/status for the directive.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Scheduling constraints
- RRULE limitations (to match the UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

#### Examples
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested create" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="suggested update" id="123" name="Daily report" prompt="Summarize Sentry errors" rrule="FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=9;BYMINUTE=0" cwds="/path/one,/path/two" status="ACTIVE"}
- ::automation-update{mode="view" id="123"}

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives after the action actually succeeds. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>
